import { PackageManagerTabs } from '@theme'

# ReactLynx (Web)

Storybook for ReactLynx Web & Rsbuild lets you develop [ReactLynx](https://lynxjs.org/) components in isolation by rendering each story through the upstream `<lynx-view>` web-core custom element.

The framework wraps your existing [`@lynx-js/rspeedy`](https://github.com/lynx-family/lynx-stack) pipeline: it reads your `lynx.config.ts`, mounts the rspeedy dev server into Storybook's own server (single origin), and dispatches each story to a registered component ‚Äî a story's `args` are spread onto the component as props.

> **Experimental.** Review the [Limitations](#limitations) before adopting.

## Requirements

<div style={{ width: '100%', maxWidth: '520px' }}>

| Package / tool | Version |
| :--- | :--- |
| An existing `@lynx-js/rspeedy` project with `pluginReactLynx()` | ‚Äî |
| `@lynx-js/rspeedy` | ‚â• 0.14.0 |
| `@lynx-js/react` | ‚â• 0.121.0 |
| `@lynx-js/web-core` | ‚â• 0.21.0 |
| `@lynx-js/web-elements` | ‚â• 0.12.0 |
| `@rsbuild/core` | ‚â• 2.0.0 |
| `storybook` | ‚â• 10.1.0 |
| Node.js | ‚â• 20.6.0 |

</div>

:::info Browser support
Matches `@lynx-js/web-core`: **Chrome ‚â• 92, Safari ‚â• 16.4**. The preview runs WASM plus a Web Worker, so older browsers cannot render stories.
:::

## Getting started

You need an existing Lynx project ‚Äî a `lynx.config.ts` that invokes `pluginReactLynx()`. The framework renders the **web** target, so your config must build a `web` environment.

### Installation

Your Lynx project already provides `@lynx-js/react`, `@lynx-js/rspeedy`, and `@lynx-js/react-rsbuild-plugin`. Add the framework and the web runtime (`@rsbuild/core` is listed directly so version pins and lockfile audits stay unambiguous):

<PackageManagerTabs command="install storybook-react-lynx-web-rsbuild @lynx-js/web-core @lynx-js/web-elements @rsbuild/core -D" />

### Configure `.storybook/main.ts`

```ts
import type { StorybookConfig } from 'storybook-react-lynx-web-rsbuild'

const config: StorybookConfig = {
  stories: ['../src/**/*.stories.@(js|jsx|ts|tsx)'],
  addons: ['@storybook/addon-docs'],
  framework: {
    name: 'storybook-react-lynx-web-rsbuild',
    options: {},
  },
}

export default config
```

### Configure `lynx.config.ts`

This is your ordinary `@lynx-js/rspeedy` config ‚Äî the **same one** `rspeedy dev` / `rspeedy build` use. The framework reads it but never rewrites it; it only injects a synthetic `__storybook__` dispatcher entry onto a private clone, so your plugins, environments, and `output` settings all apply exactly as they do outside Storybook.

```ts
import { pluginReactLynx } from '@lynx-js/react-rsbuild-plugin'
import { defineConfig } from '@lynx-js/rspeedy'

export default defineConfig({
  plugins: [pluginReactLynx()],
  environments: {
    web: {},
    lynx: {},
  },
})
```

:::warning The `web` environment is required
This framework renders the web target. If your config declares an `environments` map, it **must** include a `web` key (an empty `web: {}` is fine). A config with no `environments` block at all is also fine ‚Äî rspeedy synthesizes a default web environment. Otherwise Storybook fails to start with a branded error telling you the framework found environments but none named `web`.

You do **not** add a `source.entry` per component ‚Äî the framework injects its own dispatcher entry. Any `source.entry` you already have (e.g. your app's `main`) is preserved.
:::

### Register components in `.storybook/lynx-preview.tsx`

Create `.storybook/lynx-preview.tsx` and register every component you want stories to reference. Pass each component **directly** ‚Äî there are no `() => <Button />` wrappers and no manual prop threading:

```tsx
// .storybook/lynx-preview.tsx
import { createLynxStorybook } from 'storybook-react-lynx-web-rsbuild/runtime'

import { Button } from '../src/components/Button.tsx'
import { Card } from '../src/components/Card.tsx'

createLynxStorybook({
  components: { Button, Card },
})
```

Each component imports its own styles (e.g. `import './Button.css'` inside `Button.tsx`), so the dispatcher itself needs no CSS import.

### Write a story

Reference a registered component by name with `parameters.lynx.component`; the story's `args` flow to it as props:

```ts
// src/components/Button.stories.ts
import type { Meta, StoryObj } from 'storybook-react-lynx-web-rsbuild'

const meta = {
  title: 'Example/Button',
  parameters: {
    lynx: { component: 'Button' },
  },
  argTypes: {
    label: { control: 'text' },
    primary: { control: 'boolean' },
  },
} satisfies Meta

export default meta
type Story = StoryObj

export const Primary: Story = {
  args: { primary: true, label: 'Button' },
}
```

```tsx
// src/components/Button.tsx ‚Äî args arrive as ordinary props
export function Button(
  { label = 'Button', primary = false }: { label?: string; primary?: boolean },
) {
  // `label` / `primary` come straight from the story's `args`.
}
```

### Run

Add the standard Storybook scripts and start the dev server:

```json
{
  "scripts": {
    "storybook": "storybook dev",
    "build-storybook": "storybook build"
  }
}
```

<PackageManagerTabs command="exec storybook dev" />

The first start **blocks while rspeedy compiles the `.web.bundle`** ‚Äî this is expected, not a hang. Once it's ready, your story renders inside a `<lynx-view>` and Storybook Controls update it live.

For a complete runnable reference, see the [`react-lynx-web` sandbox](https://github.com/rstackjs/storybook-rsbuild/tree/main/sandboxes/react-lynx-web).

## Three configuration worlds

This framework spans **three** configuration surfaces. Knowing which paradigm each one is removes the "do I set this in Lynx-style or Rsbuild-style?" ambiguity.

| Surface | Style | What it controls |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| `lynx.config.ts` | **Lynx / rspeedy** (`defineConfig` + `pluginReactLynx`) | How your **component** `.web.bundle` is compiled ‚Äî loaders, plugins, environments, `output`. Reused untouched. |
| `.storybook/main.ts` ‚Üí `rsbuildFinal` / `tools.rspack` | **Rsbuild / Rspack** | How Storybook's **preview shell** is built. The builder is `storybook-builder-rsbuild`, the same as the rest of this project. |
| `.storybook/main.ts` ‚Üí `framework` / `options` / `stories` / `addons` | **Storybook** | Storybook itself ‚Äî which framework, framework options, where stories live, addons. |

**Rule of thumb:** if your **component code** imports or uses it (an alias it resolves, a loader it needs, a define it reads), configure it in **`lynx.config.ts`**. If it only affects **Storybook's own preview page**, use **`rsbuildFinal`**.

These are two different Rspack compilations: your `lynx.config.ts` builds the component `.web.bundle`; `rsbuildFinal` / `tools.rspack` tune the preview shell that hosts it.

### Where does X go?

| You want to‚Ä¶ | Configure it in‚Ä¶ |
| :--- | :--- |
| Add a Lynx loader / plugin, change `output.distPath`, add an environment | `lynx.config.ts` (Lynx / rspeedy) |
| Resolve an alias your **component** imports | `lynx.config.ts` |
| Tweak the Storybook **preview** build (alias / define / plugins for the preview shell) | `rsbuildFinal` in `main.ts` (Rsbuild) |
| Set a framework option (`lynxConfigPath`, `lynxBundlePrefix`, lazy compilation) | `framework.options` in `main.ts` (Storybook) |

### Deep build tuning (`rsbuildFinal` / `tools.rspack`)

Because the builder is `storybook-builder-rsbuild`, you tune the Storybook **preview** build exactly as anywhere else in this project ‚Äî with `rsbuildFinal` and `tools.rspack` in `.storybook/main.ts`. See the [Configuration guide](/guide/configuration#framework-options).

```ts
import type { StorybookConfig } from 'storybook-react-lynx-web-rsbuild'
import { mergeRsbuildConfig } from '@rsbuild/core'

const config: StorybookConfig = {
  framework: {
    name: 'storybook-react-lynx-web-rsbuild',
    options: {},
  },
  stories: ['../src/**/*.stories.@(js|jsx|ts|tsx)'],
  rsbuildFinal: (config) => {
    // Customize the Storybook PREVIEW build here ‚Äî not the Lynx .web.bundle,
    // which is built by your lynx.config.ts.
    return mergeRsbuildConfig(config, {
      // your custom config
    })
  },
}

export default config
```

## Framework Options

These live in `.storybook/main.ts` under `framework.options`.

### `lynxConfigPath`

Path to your rspeedy/lynx config file, relative to the project root. Defaults to `lynx.config.ts` (also tries `.js`, `.mts`, `.mjs`).

```ts
framework: {
  name: 'storybook-react-lynx-web-rsbuild',
  options: {
    lynxConfigPath: 'config/lynx.config.ts',
  },
}
```

### `lynxBundlePrefix`

URL prefix under which compiled `.web.bundle` files are served in a **production** build. Defaults to `/lynx-bundles`. The dispatcher picks this up automatically; stories that pass an explicit `parameters.lynx.url` must include the matching prefix themselves. (In dev the bundle is served from the mounted rspeedy server's root, so this prefix does not apply there.) Most projects never set this ‚Äî change it only if `/lynx-bundles` collides with another route in your deployment.

### `builder.lazyCompilation`

The framework disables Rsbuild lazy compilation in dev by default, because it breaks `@lynx-js/web-core`'s WASM worker (the async-wasm runtime helper is never installed in the worker, crashing dev with `__webpack_require__.v is not a function`). For a large component library you can opt back in:

```ts
framework: {
  name: 'storybook-react-lynx-web-rsbuild',
  options: {
    builder: { lazyCompilation: true },
  },
}
```

`builder` is a `storybook-builder-rsbuild` option surfaced here ‚Äî it tunes the **preview** build, not your rspeedy build.

## Escape hatches

| Hatch | When to reach for it |
| :--- | :--- |
| `parameters.lynx.url` | Point a story at a hand-hosted / remote `.web.bundle` instead of the dispatcher. |
| `framework.options.lynxConfigPath` | Your `lynx.config.*` is not at the project root or uses a non-default name. |
| `framework.options.lynxBundlePrefix` | The default `/lynx-bundles` production URL prefix collides with another route. |
| `framework.options.builder.lazyCompilation` | A large component library where you want lazy compilation back on. |
| `rsbuildFinal` / `tools.rspack` | Deep Rsbuild/Rspack tuning of the **preview** build (see [Configuration](/guide/configuration#framework-options)). |
| Your own `lynx.config.ts` | Full rspeedy power for the **component** build ‚Äî custom plugins, extra environments, `output.distPath`, etc. The framework leaves it intact. |
| `useGlobalProps()` from `@lynx-js/react` | A component that reads the raw `globalProps` bag instead of args-as-props. |

### `parameters.lynx.url`

For advanced cases ‚Äî remote bundles, custom prefixes, or a bundle hosted outside the framework ‚Äî point `parameters.lynx.url` at the bundle directly. Prefer `component:` for day-to-day use.

```ts
parameters: { lynx: { url: 'https://cdn.example.com/my.web.bundle' } }
```

Resolution order is `url` ‚Üí `component`. If the active `component:` is not registered in `.storybook/lynx-preview.tsx`, the runtime dispatcher renders a **visible error listing the registered component names** (so a typo is obvious); if the whole `.storybook/lynx-preview.*` file is missing, the preview shows an inline error telling you where to create it. Pass a `fallback` to `createLynxStorybook({ components, fallback })` to render your own placeholder for the unselected/unknown cases instead.

### `useGlobalProps()`

Components that prefer to read the raw `globalProps` bag (instead of taking args as props) can still call `useGlobalProps()` from `@lynx-js/react` ‚Äî the dispatcher leaves the bag intact. Augment the `GlobalProps` interface for type safety:

```tsx
import { useGlobalProps } from '@lynx-js/react'

declare module '@lynx-js/react' {
  interface GlobalProps {
    label?: string
    primary?: boolean
  }
}
```

## Features

- Runs your own `@lynx-js/rspeedy` pipeline in-process and mounts its dev server directly into Storybook's own server ‚Äî single origin, no sidecar process and no reverse proxy to manage.
- Editing a **story** soft-updates through Storybook's own HMR ‚Äî no full page reload; the `<lynx-view>` re-renders with the new args/parameters.
- Editing **component source** live-reloads the preview to pick up the rebuilt bundle (the `web` target has no React Fast-Refresh, so a rebuild is a reload).
- Tweaking a control updates via `updateGlobalProps()` without remounting ‚Äî component state survives arg changes.
- Production build via `storybook build` that runs `rspeedy build` in-process and copies `.web.bundle` plus static assets into the Storybook output.

## Limitations

- **No Docs mode inline rendering.** ReactLynx components only run inside a `<lynx-view>` with WASM + Worker; docs pages that embed stories will render the empty custom element host.
- **No `play()` interaction tests yet.** The component lives inside a shadow root and a cross-thread worker; upstream test harnesses need more work.
- **No automatic args docgen.** `react-docgen` does not understand the ReactLynx transforms.
- **Each story spawns a Worker.** Lynx runs background-thread JS in a dedicated Web Worker; switching between many stories in one session will accumulate memory pressure.
- **Browser requirements match `@lynx-js/web-core`:** Chrome ‚â• 92, Safari ‚â• 16.4.
- **Storybook must be served from the origin root.** The preview pins its asset `publicPath` to `/` (web-core's worker resolves shared chunks by absolute path), so a `storybook build` deployed under a sub-path ‚Äî e.g. `https://host/storybook/` ‚Äî would request preview assets from `/static/‚Ä¶` and 404. Deploy the static output at the root, or front it with a rewrite.

## Troubleshooting

### `No lynx.config.{ts,js,mts,mjs} found`

The framework needs your Lynx config at the project root, or set `framework.options.lynxConfigPath`. Create one that invokes `pluginReactLynx()`.

### `environments { ‚Ä¶ } but none named 'web'`

Your `lynx.config.ts` declares an `environments` map without a `web` key. Add `web: {}` ‚Äî this framework renders the web target.

### A story shows "Unknown component ‚Ä¶"

`parameters.lynx.component` does not match a key you passed to `createLynxStorybook({ components })`. The error panel lists the registered names ‚Äî check for a typo, or register the component.

### Blank `<lynx-view>` / story doesn't render

Confirm the first compile finished (the dev server blocks on it) and that your browser meets the floor (Chrome ‚â• 92 / Safari ‚â• 16.4).

- **[Example Sandbox](https://github.com/rstackjs/storybook-rsbuild/tree/main/sandboxes/react-lynx-web)**: A complete working setup.
